answers:
**1. Where should new refund provider calls live, and does the code already follow that pattern?**

New refund calls belong in `backend/app/billing/gateway.py` (medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-17), alongside `charge()` and `create_customer()` — this is the module the codebase's own docstring (line 3-4) and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7) both name as "the single place the chargekit provider client is built." You'd add a `refund(tenant_id, charge_reference, amount_cents=None)`-style accessor there that calls `_client.refund(...)`, and callers (an invoice API route, a worker) would import that accessor rather than touching `chargekit` themselves.

The current code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere. Two places bypass `gateway.py` and talk to the provider directly:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4,14` — `charge_invoice()` does `import chargekit` and builds its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` instead of calling `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2,9` — `run_dunning_pass()` does the same: `import chargekit` and instantiates `chargekit.Client(...)` inline instead of using `gateway.charge()`.

The only sanctioned exception is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6`, which imports just `chargekit.errors` for exception types and explicitly documents itself as the boundary exception. `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` are not on that exception list and are undocumented violations of the stated invariant. If refund support is added by writing directly into `invoices.py` (mirroring the existing `charge_invoice()` code), it will silently perpetuate this violation rather than fix it.

**2. Where does `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` no longer match the code?**

- **Payment integration boundary** (doc line 5-7: "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py"). Actual `grep -rn "chargekit" --include="*.py" .` shows four files reference `chargekit`, not two: `gateway.py`, `errors.py` (both documented), plus `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` (undocumented, direct `chargekit.Client` construction — see answer 1). The stated invariant is currently false.
- **Invoice API response shape** (doc line 11-12: "contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status"). The actual response, `InvoiceOut` in `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22`, has six fields — the five listed plus `external_ref`. That field was added in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices") via migration `0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql`, without updating the architecture doc. The word "exactly" makes this a hard factual error, not just an omission.
- **Webhook event types** (see answer 4 below) — also stale.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when?**

Per the skill's core rule ("drift between it and the code is treated as a bug, not as 'the doc needs updating eventually'"), both existing discrepancies should have been fixed already, in the same commits that caused them — `c23da88` should have updated the Invoice API section to list `external_ref`, and whichever commit added the `payment_pending` webhook branch should have updated the Webhooks section. Since neither happened, the fix is now overdue technical debt and should be corrected before or alongside starting the refund feature, not bundled invisibly into the refund PR: (a) update the Invoice API section to include `external_ref`, and (b) update the Webhooks section to reflect the actual handled types (see answer 4). Then, when refund support is implemented, the same commit/PR that adds `gateway.refund()` must update the doc again if it adds any new webhook types (e.g., `refund_failed`) or changes the invoice response shape, and that update must be called out explicitly in the PR description per the skill's guidance. The two existing direct-`chargekit` call sites (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) should also either be routed through `gateway.py` or added to an explicit, justified exception list in the doc — leaving them as silent, undocumented drift is exactly the failure mode the skill warns against.

**4. Assess the webhook event-types claim.**

The doc claims (medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17): "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."

The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20`, branches on five types: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened` — plus a fallback `{"handled": None}` for anything else. So the doc both undercounts (says 4, code handles 5) and omits `payment_pending` entirely.

This is exactly the "don't cite a number: read the source" trap the skill describes — a hardcoded count that drifted the moment someone added `payment_pending` without updating the doc. I would change the wording to drop the count and either enumerate the current list accurately (`payment_succeeded, payment_failed, payment_pending, refund_created, dispute_opened`) or, better, describe the property instead of listing/counting: e.g. "the integration handles a known allow-list of webhook event types in `app/api/webhooks.py`; unrecognized types are accepted and marked unhandled." That formulation survives the next event type being added without going stale, and pairs well with adding refund-related webhook types (e.g. a `refund_failed` event) as part of this feature.
report:
I have everything needed. Let me compile the final report.

## Plan

The task was to review a proposed refund-support feature against the medibill backend's existing payment-provider integration and its `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, strictly read-only. I first read `.claude/skills/architecture-contracts-as-law/SKILL.md`, which frames the exercise: a single documented boundary module for third-party SDK calls is supposed to be enforceable via `grep`, and any place where the code deviates from the documented invariant is a violation to be listed explicitly (an honest exception list, not silent drift). I then needed to locate the actual target repo, since the initial working directory (`clinic-scheduler`) has no `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` and isn't a payments backend at all.

## Actions

- Read `.claude/skills/architecture-contracts-as-law/SKILL.md` in full — its "Isolate third-party SDK / vendor boundaries to one module" section is the guide for question 1 (funnel provider calls through one module; grep for violations; maintain an explicit exception list).
- `Glob **/*` from the initial working directory — showed only `clinic-scheduler` (a scheduling app with Alembic migrations, no payments code, no `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`).
- Read `clinic-scheduler` root `README.md` — no ARCHITECTURE reference.
- `grep -rl "ARCHITECTURE"` across `/…/fixtures/saasapp/` — found matches only in `medibill/` (`errors.py`, `README.md`).
- Read `/…/work/…/README.md` (workspace index) — confirms `medibill/` is "clinic scheduling SaaS billing/payments backend," a separate repo from `clinic-scheduler` and already present in the work directory.
- `find` on `medibill/` (excluding `.git`) — enumerated all files: `backend/app/api/invoices.py`, `backend/app/api/webhooks.py`, `backend/app/billing/errors.py`, `backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, `backend/app/models/invoice.py`, `backend/app/workers/dunning.py`, migrations `0001`–`0006`, `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, `README.md`, `frontend/src/App.jsx`, `infra/main.tf`, `tests/test_webhooks.py`.
- Read `medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (17 lines) — states the payment-integration boundary, the invoice API response shape, and the webhook event types handled.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py` — the documented single provider-call module; wraps `chargekit.Client` with `charge()` and `create_customer()`; no `refund()` function exists yet.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client` inside `charge_invoice()`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py` — handles five event kinds: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py` — imports only `chargekit.errors` exception types, and its own docstring calls this out as "the documented boundary exception (see docs/ARCHITECTURE.md)."
- Read `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py` — `Invoice`/`InvoiceOut` dataclasses carry six fields: `id`, `tenant_id`, `amount_cents`, `currency`, `status`, `external_ref`.
- Read `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py` — imports `chargekit` directly and constructs its own `chargekit.Client` inside `run_dunning_pass()`.
- Ran `grep -rn "chargekit" --include="*.py" .` inside `medibill/` — confirmed exactly four files reference `chargekit`: `gateway.py` (the documented module), `errors.py` (documented exception), and two undocumented direct users, `invoices.py` and `dunning.py`.
- Read `backend/migrations/0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql` — `ALTER TABLE invoice ADD COLUMN external_ref TEXT;`.
- Read `tests/test_webhooks.py` — only a placeholder test (`assert True`), no real coverage of webhook handling.
- Ran `git --no-pager log --oneline -15` inside `medibill/` — three commits: `23d2f37` "MEDB-180 initial billing backend and architecture doc", `3a7daee` "MEDB-197 nightly dunning retry worker", `c23da88` "MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices". The `external_ref` field and its migration were added in a later commit than the architecture doc, without an accompanying doc update.
- Read `medibill/README.md` — confirms `chargekit` is the payment provider SDK and points to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` as the source of truth.

## Answers

**1. Where should new refund provider calls live, and does the code already follow that pattern?**

New refund calls belong in `backend/app/billing/gateway.py` (medibill/backend/app/billing/gateway.py:1-17), alongside `charge()` and `create_customer()` — this is the module the codebase's own docstring (line 3-4) and `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` (medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:5-7) both name as "the single place the chargekit provider client is built." You'd add a `refund(tenant_id, charge_reference, amount_cents=None)`-style accessor there that calls `_client.refund(...)`, and callers (an invoice API route, a worker) would import that accessor rather than touching `chargekit` themselves.

The current code does **not** follow that pattern everywhere. Two places bypass `gateway.py` and talk to the provider directly:

- `medibill/backend/app/api/invoices.py:4,14` — `charge_invoice()` does `import chargekit` and builds its own `chargekit.Client(api_key_env="CHARGEKIT_API_KEY")` instead of calling `gateway.charge()`.
- `medibill/backend/app/workers/dunning.py:2,9` — `run_dunning_pass()` does the same: `import chargekit` and instantiates `chargekit.Client(...)` inline instead of using `gateway.charge()`.

The only sanctioned exception is `medibill/backend/app/billing/errors.py:6`, which imports just `chargekit.errors` for exception types and explicitly documents itself as the boundary exception. `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` are not on that exception list and are undocumented violations of the stated invariant. If refund support is added by writing directly into `invoices.py` (mirroring the existing `charge_invoice()` code), it will silently perpetuate this violation rather than fix it.

**2. Where does `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` no longer match the code?**

- **Payment integration boundary** (doc line 5-7: "Every call to the payment provider goes through app/billing/gateway.py; the only exception is app/billing/errors.py"). Actual `grep -rn "chargekit" --include="*.py" .` shows four files reference `chargekit`, not two: `gateway.py`, `errors.py` (both documented), plus `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` (undocumented, direct `chargekit.Client` construction — see answer 1). The stated invariant is currently false.
- **Invoice API response shape** (doc line 11-12: "contains exactly the fields: id, tenant_id, amount_cents, currency, status"). The actual response, `InvoiceOut` in `medibill/backend/app/models/invoice.py:16-22`, has six fields — the five listed plus `external_ref`. That field was added in commit `c23da88` ("MEDB-214 record external payment ref on invoices") via migration `0006_add_invoice_external_ref.sql`, without updating the architecture doc. The word "exactly" makes this a hard factual error, not just an omission.
- **Webhook event types** (see answer 4 below) — also stale.

**3. What should happen to `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`, and when?**

Per the skill's core rule ("drift between it and the code is treated as a bug, not as 'the doc needs updating eventually'"), both existing discrepancies should have been fixed already, in the same commits that caused them — `c23da88` should have updated the Invoice API section to list `external_ref`, and whichever commit added the `payment_pending` webhook branch should have updated the Webhooks section. Since neither happened, the fix is now overdue technical debt and should be corrected before or alongside starting the refund feature, not bundled invisibly into the refund PR: (a) update the Invoice API section to include `external_ref`, and (b) update the Webhooks section to reflect the actual handled types (see answer 4). Then, when refund support is implemented, the same commit/PR that adds `gateway.refund()` must update the doc again if it adds any new webhook types (e.g., `refund_failed`) or changes the invoice response shape, and that update must be called out explicitly in the PR description per the skill's guidance. The two existing direct-`chargekit` call sites (`invoices.py`, `dunning.py`) should also either be routed through `gateway.py` or added to an explicit, justified exception list in the doc — leaving them as silent, undocumented drift is exactly the failure mode the skill warns against.

**4. Assess the webhook event-types claim.**

The doc claims (medibill/docs/ARCHITECTURE.md:16-17): "The integration handles 4 webhook event types: payment_succeeded, payment_failed, refund_created, dispute_opened."

The actual handler, `medibill/backend/app/api/webhooks.py:9-20`, branches on five types: `payment_succeeded`, `payment_failed`, `payment_pending`, `refund_created`, `dispute_opened` — plus a fallback `{"handled": None}` for anything else. So the doc both undercounts (says 4, code handles 5) and omits `payment_pending` entirely.

This is exactly the "don't cite a number: read the source" trap the skill describes — a hardcoded count that drifted the moment someone added `payment_pending` without updating the doc. I would change the wording to drop the count and either enumerate the current list accurately (`payment_succeeded, payment_failed, payment_pending, refund_created, dispute_opened`) or, better, describe the property instead of listing/counting: e.g. "the integration handles a known allow-list of webhook event types in `app/api/webhooks.py`; unrecognized types are accepted and marked unhandled." That formulation survives the next event type being added without going stale, and pairs well with adding refund-related webhook types (e.g. a `refund_failed` event) as part of this feature.

## Next steps

If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) add `refund()` to `backend/app/billing/gateway.py`, mirroring the `charge()` accessor; (2) refactor `invoices.py` and `dunning.py` to call `gateway.charge()` instead of constructing their own `chargekit.Client`, closing the two existing boundary violations before adding new code that would otherwise copy the same anti-pattern for refunds; (3) add a refund API route/worker call that goes through `gateway.refund()` only; (4) update `docs/ARCHITECTURE.md` in the same commit to fix the invoice-field list (add `external_ref`), correct/reword the webhook section, and document the new refund call and any new webhook types it introduces; (5) add real test coverage for webhook handling and the new refund path, since `tests/test_webhooks.py` is currently just a placeholder.

## Confidence and gaps

High confidence on all four answers — each is grounded directly in file contents and a `grep` I ran myself (`chargekit` usage count, doc text vs. code text, migration history via `git log`). One gap: I don't have visibility into the `chargekit` SDK itself (it's an external/third-party package, not vendored in this repo), so I can't confirm what a `refund()` call on `chargekit.Client` actually looks like or whether it requires additional parameters (e.g., a original-charge reference) — that would need to be checked against the SDK's actual interface (e.g., installed package docs or `pip show chargekit`) before implementation. I also could not run the placeholder test suite meaningfully since it has no real assertions, so I can't say anything about current webhook test coverage beyond noting its absence.
